parties: Between Robert Landry, Petitioner, (Appellant) and The Legal Services Society, Respondent, (Respondent)
citation: [1986] B.C.J. No. 336 Also reported at 28 C.C.C. (3d) 138 (with date May 15, 1986)
body_html:
Heard: April 22, 1986
Judgment: May 12, 1986
(21 pp.)

Barristers and solicitors -- s. 3(2) -- Legal Services Society Act.

Issue: Whether Appellant, an inmate of a prison who is charged with a disciplinary offence under s. 39 of the Penitentiary Service Regulations, is entitled to have legal services provided to him under the provisions of the Legal Services Society Act?

Per Macfarlane, J.A. (per curiam): The proceedings against the Appellant under the Penitentiary Service Regulations do not fall within the description of matters under s. 3(2) of the Legal Services Society Act which would obligate the Society to provide legal services to the Appellant. The proceedings before the prison Disciplinary Court are neither criminal [s. 3(2)(a)], nor civil [s. 3(2)(b)] in nature, and the problem facing the Appellant does not threaten his livelihood [s. 3(2)(d)(iii)]. [B.C. Recent Decisions, vol. 6, no. 35.]

Counsel for the Appellant: John W. Conroy, Esq.
Counsel for the Respondent: A.D.P. MacAdams, Esq.
Counsel for the Attorney General of British Columbia: E.R.A.
Edwards, Esq., Q.C.

MACFARLANE J.A.:-- The question raised by this appeal is whether the appellant, an inmate of a prison who is charged with a disciplinary offence under s. 39 of the Penitentiary Service Regulations, C.R.C. Vol. XIII, c. 1251, made pursuant to the Penitentiary Act, R.S.C. 1970, c. P-6, is entitled to have legal services provided to him under the provisions of the Legal Services Society Act, R.S.B.C. 1979, c. 227.

Mr. Justice Meredith, in a judgment reported at [1985] 5 W.W.R. 417, held that the appellant was not entitled to legal aid. This appeal is from that decision.

Section 3 of the Legal Services Society Act provides:

3. (1) The objects of the society are to ensure that

(a) services ordinarily provided by a lawyer are afforded to individuals who would not otherwise receive them because of financial or other reasons; and
(b) education, advice and information about law are provided for the people of British Columbia.

(2) The society shall ensure, for the purposes of subsection (1)(a), that legal services are available for a qualifying individual who

(a) is a defendant in criminal proceedings that could lead to his imprisonment;
(b) may be imprisoned or confined through civil proceedings;
(c) is or may be a party to a proceeding respecting a domestic dispute that affects his physical or mental safety or health or that of his children; or
(d) has a legal problem that threatens

(i) his family's physical or mental safety or health;

(ii) his ability to feed, clothe and provide shelter for himself and his dependents; or

(iii) his livelihood. (my underlining)

The appellant would qualify for legal aid if s. 3(2)(a), (b) or (d)(iii) apply.

Mr. Justice Meredith concluded that the proceedings before the prison Disciplinary Court are neither criminal nor civil in nature, and that the problem facing the appellant does not threaten his livelihood.

The question on this appeal is not whether the appellant is entitled to legal representation before the Disciplinary Court. In Re: Howard (1985) 19 C.C.C. (3d) 195 (F.C.A.), it was held that such a person was so entitled. It is not necessary for us to consider that question. The issue in this case is whether the foregoing portions of s. 3(2) of the Legal Services Society Act apply. If they do, then the Legal Services Society would be obliged to provide legal services -- see Mountain v. Legal Services Society [1984] 2 W.W.R. 438 (B.C.C.A.).

The Penitentiary Service Regulations are made pursuant to s. 29 of the Penitentiary Act which provides:

29.(1) Regulations. -- The Governor in Council may make regulations

(a) for the organization, training, discipline, efficiency, administration and good government of the Service;
(b) for the custody, treatment, training employment and discipline of inmates;
(b.1) prescribing the compensation that may be paid pursuant to section 28.1 and the manner of its payment;
(b.2) defining the term "spouse" and the expression "dependent child" for the purposes of section 28.1;
(b.3) for the collection, administration and distribution of estates of deceased inmates; and
(c) generally, for carrying into effect the purposes and provisions of this Act.

(2) Penalty for violation. -- The Governor in Council may, in any regulations made under subsection (1) other than paragraph (b) thereof, provide for a fine not exceeding five hundred dollars or imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months, or both, to be imposed upon summary conviction for the violation of any such regulation.

(3) Rules and orders of Commissioner. -- Subject to this Act and any regulations made under subsection (1), the Commissioner may make rules, to be known as Commissioner's directives, for the organization, training, discipline, efficiency, administration and good government of the Service, and for the custody, treatment, training employment and discipline of inmates and the good government of penitentiaries. (my underlining)

The following Regulations are relevant:

Inmate Discipline

38.(1) The institutional head of each institution is responsible for the disciplinary control of inmates confined therein.

(2) No inmate shall be punished except pursuant to

(a) an order of the institutional head or an officer designated by the institutional head; or
(b) an order of a disciplinary court.

(3) Where an inmate is convicted of a disciplinary offence the punishment shall, except where the offence is flagrant or serious, consist of loss of privileges.

(4) The punishment that may be ordered for a flagrant or serious disciplinary offence shall consist of one or more of the following:

(a) forfeiture of statutory or earned remission or both;
(b) dissociation for a period not exceeding thirty days;
(c) loss of privileges.

Disciplinary Court

38.1(1)The Minister may appoint a person to preside over a disciplinary court.

(2) A person appointed pursuant to subsection (1) shall

(a) conduct the hearing;
(b) consult, in the presence of the accused inmate, with two officers designated by the institutional head;
(c) determine the guilt or innocence of an accused inmate appearing before him; and
(d) on finding an accused inmate guilty, order such punishment authorized by these Regulations as he deems suitable.

(3) The remuneration of a person appointed pursuant to subsection (1) shall be two hundred and fifty dollars for each day that he presides over a disciplinary court, plus travelling expenses in accordance with the Treasury Board travel directive.

Inmates Offences

39. Every inmate commits a disciplinary offence who

(a) disobeys or fails to obey a lawful order of a penitentiary officer,
(b) assaults or threatens to assault another person,
(c) refuses to work or fails to work to the best of his ability,
(d) leaves his work without permission of a penitentiary officer,
(e) damages government property or the property of another person,
(f) wilfully wastes food,
(g) is indecent, disrespectful or threatening in his actions, language or writing toward any other person,
(h) wilfully disobeys or fails to obey any regulation or rule governing the conduct of inmates,
(i) has contraband in his possession,
(j) deals in contraband with any other person,
(k) does any act that is calculated to prejudice the discipline or good order of the institution,
(l) does any act with intent to escape or to assist another inmate to escape,
(m) gives or offers a bribe or reward to any person for any purpose,
(n) contravenes any rule, regulation or directive made under the Act, or
(o) attempts to do anything mentioned in paragraphs (a) to (n). (my underlining)

Dissociation

40.(1) Where the institutional head is satisfied that

(a) for the maintenance of good order and discipline in the institution, or
(b) in the best interests of an inmate it is necessary or desirable that the inmate should be kept from associating with other inmates, he may order the inmate to be dissociated accordingly, but the case of every inmate so dissociated shall be considered, not less than once each month, by the Classification Board for the purpose of recommending to the institutional head whether or not the inmate should return to association with other inmates.

(2) An inmate who has been dissociated is not considered under punishment unless he has been sentenced as such and he shall not be deprived of any of his privileges and amenities by reason thereof, except those privileges and amenities that

(a) can only be enjoyed in association with other inmates, or
(b) cannot reasonably be granted having regard to the limitations of the dissociation area and the necessity for the effective operation thereof.

Offences Generally

41.(1) Every one who

(a) delivers or attempts to deliver contraband to an inmate,
(b) receives or attempts to receive contraband from an inmate,
(c) trespasses upon penitentiary lands, or
(d) assists any person to do anything mentioned in paragraph (a), (b) or (c),

is guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction and is liable to imprisonment for six months or to a fine of $500, or to both.

The appellant is charged with a breach of Regulation 39(1).

Commissioner's Directive 600.7.03.1 provides, in part:

19. Where an inmate appears to have committed a serious misconduct which clearly contravenes an act of parliament, the Director shall inform the police force having jurisdiction unless circumstances warrant otherwise.

20. If the Director or the member designated by him informs the police, charges shall not be processed through the internal disciplinary court until and unless the Crown declines to prosecute the case.

Such directives have been held not to have the force of law -- Martineau v. Matsqui Institution Inmate Disciplinary Board (No. 2) (1979) 13 C.R. (3d) 1 (S.C.C.) at p. 8, 17 and 21.

In this case, internal disciplinary proceedings were undertaken and concluded, but on appeal were set aside for reasons which are not relevant here.

There is no question in this appeal arising under s. 11(h) of the Charter as there have been no proceedings in the ordinary criminal courts.

Is the appellant a defendant in criminal proceedings that could lead to his imprisonment?

Mr. Justice Meredith held, at p. 418:

"Forfeiture of remission would, I think, constitute 'proceedings that could lead to the imprisonment' of the petitioner within the meaning of the Legal Services Society Act. Furthermore, 'dissociation' has been described as confinement to a 'prison within a prison'. From this standpoint, the proceedings could also be construed to possibly lead to the imprisonment of the petitioner within the prison itself. But in order to qualify under the Legal Services Society Act, the petitioner must be faced with either criminal or civil proceedings. Here he is faced with neither."

The appellant takes issue only with the judge's finding that the proceedings are neither criminal or civil, and that they did not threaten his livelihood.

On the question whether the Disciplinary Court proceedings are criminal proceedings, the appellant submits:

1. The proceedings under the Penitentiary Service Regulations ought to be characterized as criminal, using that term in its widest sense.

2. The charge against the appellant is "substantially similar, if not identical" to a charge of escape under s. 133, or an attempt under s. 421 of the Criminal Code.

3. The procedure provided for in the Commissioner's Directives is highly analogous to the procedure under the Criminal Code in that a charge is preferred, a plea is taken, there is a right to a full hearing and to counsel, there is a right of examination and cross-examination, proof must be beyond a reasonable doubt, and penal consequences may follow. Furthermore, the sentencing provisions of the Regulations contemplate a possible deprivation of liberty.

The respondent, and the intervenor, submit:

1. The character of the proceedings before the Disciplinary Court is to be determined upon a construction of the statute or law under which the appellant is charged.
2. The proceedings are not criminal or civil, but disciplinary, the purpose being to enforce the disciplinary code provided for by regulation, to maintain discipline and order within the prison, and to ensure the good government of penitentiaries. The purpose of s. 133 and s. 421 of the Criminal Code is to enforce the general penal law. It is invoked by the appropriate Attorney General, and is dealt with in the ordinary courts.
3. The procedural trappings of a disciplinary proceeding, while analogous to those found in regular criminal proceedings, are required to meet the demands of procedural fairness.

It must always be a question of the construction of the particular statute whether an act is prohibited in the sense that it is rendered criminal, or whether the statute merely affixes certain consequences more or less unpleasant to the doing of the act. The nature and character of the proceedings is not determined by the result, but rather by the law upon which the proceedings are based, or out of which they arise -- Re Storgoff [1945] S.C.R. 526, at 543, 594, 598.

There is nothing in the Legal Services Society Act which indicates that it was contemplated that the Act would apply to disciplinary proceedings. Can the proceedings under the Penitentiary Service Regulations be characterized as criminal proceedings?

Section 29 of the Penitentiary Act empowers the Governor in Council to make regulations (b) for the custody, treatment, training, employment and discipline of inmates.

In contrast is the power under s. 29(2) of the Act which provides for a fine not exceeding $500 or imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months or both, to be imposed upon summary conviction for the violation of any regulation. Such punishment is provided in the Regulations for those who deliver contraband to an inmate, trespass upon penitentiary lands or are involved in those activities. It does not apply to a disciplinary offence committed by an inmate. Thus, a line is drawn between a summary conviction offence (a criminal charge), and a disciplinary offence.

The power to make regulations and rules is directed primarily at maintaining the good government of penitentiaries. Discipline of inmates is viewed in the same light as matters pertaining to the custody, treatment, training and employment of inmates. The power which is given to the Commissioner to make rules is directed at achieving the same internal objectives.

Regulation 38 entitled "Inmate Discipline" and which is directed at "the disciplinary control of inmates" speaks of a "disciplinary offence". Punishment is confined to a loss of privileges except in cases of a flagrant or serious disciplinary offence which may involve forfeiture of statutory or earned remission, or both, or dissociation.

Regulation 39 entitled "Inmates Offences" is directed at those inmates who commit a "disciplinary offence". The Regulation is directed at a wide range of activity which would, if not controlled, lead to breakdown in order and in the good government of the prison.

Regulation 40 entitled "Dissociation" provides that an inmate may be kept from associating with other inmates but only:

(1) Where the institutional head is satisfied that

(a) for the maintenance of good order and discipline in the institution, or
(b) in the best interests of an inmate

it is necessary or desirable that the inmate should be kept from associating with other inmates ..."

It is to be noted that Regulation 40 is directed, not at the punishment of the inmate, but at the maintenance of good order and discipline in the institution. In the alternative, it is directed towards the best interests of the inmate (presumably for his protection).

Regulation 41 provides for enforcing certain regulations by the summary conviction procedure.

It is also open to the Attorney General to prosecute a charge in a court having criminal jurisdiction. That is recognized by Directive 600.7.03.1.

In this case, criminal proceedings could have been instituted under s. 133 or s. 421 of the Code, based on an escape or attempt to escape, but that course was not pursued.

In summary, the Penitentiary Act, and the Regulations made pursuant to it, recognize that criminal sanctions may be required in some areas, and that disciplinary actions may be requisite in controlling other problems. The consequences of either course may be unpleasant for the transgressor, but it is not that unhappy result which characterizes the proceedings as criminal.

An inmate disciplinary board (now called a Disciplinary Court) is not a court. It discharges what is essentially an administrative task. It is amenable to certiorari, and its procedure is subject to a duty of fairness -- see Martineau (No. 2) -- per Dickson, J. (now C.J.C.), at p. 35. But, in my opinion, that does not mean to say that a Disciplinary Court is converted into a court exercising criminal jurisdiction. A breach of the disciplinary code is not a criminal charge when tried by the Disciplinary Court. The aim and purpose of the Regulations, in the context of this case, is to maintain discipline of inmates, to maintain order in the prison, and to ensure the good government of penitentiaries. The general penal law is not invoked under the Regulations. The Regulations are not invoked at the instance of the appropriate Attorney General, as is the case in criminal proceedings. Rather, the Regulations are invoked at the instance of an administrative official. The Regulations are not invoked for the protection of the public or for the general good of society, but for an institutional purpose. In that sense, the proceedings are not criminal in their nature.

The appellant submits that the "procedural trappings" are so analogous to those available in criminal cases that the proceedings ought to be regarded as criminal proceedings. The "procedural trappings" are prescribed by Commissioners' directives, which are administrative rules, not rules of law. They are not prescribed because the criminal law demands that such procedures be applied. They are prescribed to ensure that the procedure is appropriate to meet the demands of procedural fairness. Those demands may be made on a tribunal, whether it exercise criminal or civil jurisdiction, or whether it exercise quasi-judicial or administrative functions. The application of a procedure which is analogous to criminal procedure does not mean that these proceedings are criminal.

Mr. Justice Meredith relied upon Regina v. Mingo et al. (1982) 2 C.C.C. (3d) 23 (B.C.S.C.). There, the accused were charged with offences of mischief in relation to public property and wilfully setting a fire contrary to s. 387 and s. 389 of the Criminal Code as the result of a disturbance at the penitentiary in which they were serving sentences. They were also charged prior to the trial and found guilty of several disciplinary offences arising out of the same facts. The consequences of the latter convictions were the same as in this case. The accused applied for a stay of proceedings of the criminal charges, and their application was dismissed. Toy, J. held that internal disciplinary proceedings taken against the accused by prison officials are properly characterized as administrative acts and, accordingly, that there was no violation of s. 11(h) of the Charter by initiation of subsequent criminal proceedings. In the course of his oral reasons for judgment, Toy, J. referred to the dicta of Dickson, J. in Martineau (No. 2), at p. 35, to which I have already referred. He held, in essence, that there was a distinction to be drawn between disciplinary proceedings and criminal proceedings. He applied and followed R. v. Hogan [1960] 2 Q.B. 513 and R. v. Hull Prison Board of Visitors Ex Parte St. Germain et al. [1979] 1 All E.R. 701. In both cases, a clear distinction was drawn between matters of internal discipline and criminal matters. In the latter case, there had been a riot in a prison. Disciplinary charges had been laid against certain inmates, and a board had heard and determined those charges. The inmates applied to the Divisional Court of the Queen's Bench for certiorari. The Court held that certiorari was not available. There was an appeal to the Court of Appeal which would only have jurisdiction if the order appealed from was not a judgment in a criminal cause or matter. The Court held that the offences against the prison Rules were offences against discipline in a code of private law which applied to a limited class of persons and were not offences against public law tried before a criminal tribunal at which an offender was liable to be convicted of a criminal offence and punished for such an offence. It was not, therefore, a criminal cause or matter. But, nevertheless, the Court concluded, as the Supreme Court of Canada did in Martineau, that the board had a duty to act judicially, and that the decisions they made were subject to judicial review by way of certiorari.

The appellant submits that Mr. Justice Toy was wrong in Mingo when he said that a Disciplinary Court is "a private or domestic court, exclusively maintained to adjudicate upon inmates' disciplinary offences and to order the restricted punishments of loss of earned remission and punitive segregation". It is submitted on the basis of what was said in Martineau (No. 2) that a Disciplinary Court is a public body performing a public duty affecting citizens, and is not a private or domestic tribunal. The statement of Mr. Justice Toy must be viewed in relation to his consideration of R. v. Hull Prison Board of Visitors Ex Parte St. Germain et al. It was in that context that Mr. Justice Toy described the Disciplinary Court as a private or domestic court. In that context, and in that sense, there was no conflict with what was said in Martineau (No. 2).

Mr. Justice Meredith also cited Regina v. Wigglesworth (1984) 11 C.C.C. (3d) 27 (Sask. C.A.). The question in that case was whether, in the light of the double jeopardy provisions of the Charter (s. 11(h)), a policeman who was tried and punished under the Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act for unnecessary violence towards a prisoner, may be tried and punished under the Criminal Code for common assault arising out of the same incident. Cameron, J.A., giving the judgment of the Court, held that there was a distinction to be drawn between the disciplinary proceedings and the criminal proceedings and that the constable could be tried and punished for each.

In coming to that conclusion, he said, at p. 32-33:

"A single act may have more than one aspect, and it may give rise to more than one legal consequence. It may, if it constitutes a breach of the duty a person owes to society, amount to a crime, for which the actor must answer to the public. At the same time, the act may, if it involves injury and a breach of one's duty to another, constitute a private cause of action for damages for which the actor must answer to the person he injured. And that same act may have still another aspect to it: it may also involve a breach of the duties of one's office or calling, in which event the actor must account to his professional peers. For example, a doctor who sexually assaults a patient will be liable, at one and the same time, to a criminal conviction at the behest of the State; to a judgment for damages, at the instance of the patient, and to an order of discipline on the motion of the governing council of his profession. Similarly, a policeman who assaults a prisoner is answerable to the State for his crime; to the victim for the damage he caused, and to the police force for discipline.

This has long been the law, and nothing, in my respectful opinion, in s. 11(h) of the Charter has changed matters in this respect. In the light of this I think Constable Wigglesworth's contention must fail since the proceeding before the R.C.M.P. service tribunal was purely disciplinary. It was concerned only with the professional aspect of his conduct: the 'offence' of which he was found guilty, a 'major service offence', lay in the breach by him of his policeman's duty not to treat his prisoners harshly, cruelly, or with unnecessary violence. He must still answer to society for the criminal aspect of his conduct, or for his 'criminal offence'."

I adopt those cases as illustrations of the difference in the character of disciplinary proceedings and criminal proceedings.

Whether disciplinary proceedings are to be stayed in the face of a conviction for substantially the same offence, containing the same elements, and arising out of the same set of circumstances, is another question. It involves the meaning to be given to the word "offence" in s. 11 of the Charter. There is conflict in the cases on that point.

In Re: Morin and National S.H.U. Review Committee et al. (1985) 20 C.C.C. (3d) 123 (F.C.A.), McGuigan, J., at p. 147, observed "that correctional proceedings such as these fall between the traditional criminal and civil spheres." He held that such proceedings were more analogous to criminal law than to civil law, and that the acquittal of an inmate on a charge under the Criminal Code was a bar to a charge, based on the same facts, under the prison Regulations.

Re: Peltari and Director of the Lower Mainland Regional Correctional Centre et al. (1985) 15 C.C.C. (3d) 223 (B.C.S.C.) is a case where the accused was charged with being unlawfully at large from a provincial correctional centre contrary to the Criminal Code. Following a trial in Provincial Court, he was found not guilty. He was then charged under the prison Regulations and was found guilty and sentenced to a loss of 30 days remission. He applied for a review of the decision of the disciplinary panel on the basis that his rights as guaranteed by s. 11(h) of the Charter had been infringed. Gibbs, J. held that for the purposes of s. 11(h), the word "offence" means conduct prohibited by law on pain of punishment. He held that the offences are the same under the Code and in the disciplinary proceedings, they contain the same elements and constitute one and the same offence arising out of the same set of circumstances. He did not decide that the proceedings before the Disciplinary Court were criminal proceedings.

Re: Russell et al. and Radley (1984) 11 C.C.C. (3d) 289 (F.C.T.D.) was a case where the trial of an inmate on serious or flagrant disciplinary offences had been repeatedly adjourned and delayed. He applied for an order in the nature of prohibition restraining the Disciplinary Court from continuing what the proceedings against him on the ground that his right guaranteed by s. 11(b) of the Charter had been infringed (the right to be tried within a reasonable time). Muldoon, J. said, at p. 306, that the word "offence", as used in s. 11, "must mean conduct (truly, culpable misconduct) defined and prohibited by law, which, if found beyond a reasonable doubt to have been committed in fact, is punishable by fine, imprisonment or other penalty imposed according to law upon the culpable miscreant, the offender." Applying that standard, he said that a disciplinary offence defined in the Penitentiary Service Regulations is an offence within the meaning of s. 11 of the Charter. He held that the Charter applied and granted the order of prohibition.

None of those cases hold that disciplinary proceedings under the Penitentiary Service Regulations are criminal proceedings, although there is no doubt that they are characterized as analogous thereto, or penal in nature. It is not necessary on this appeal to resolve the conflict of opinion over what constitutes an "offence" within the meaning of s. 11 of the Charter. The question is not whether the charge against the appellant is "an offence" within the meaning of s. 11 of the Charter. The Legal Services Society Act does not ensure legal aid for a person who "is charged with an offence that could lead to his imprisonment". Such general language might bring the legislation within the ambit of the cases to which I have referred, but the language of the statute is more specific. Coverage under s. 3(2)(a) is limited to a defendant in criminal proceedings.

In R. v. Cohn (1984) 4 O.A.C. 293, while holding that contempt in the face of the Court during a criminal trial was criminal contempt of court, Martin, J.A., at p. 301, had this to say about s. 11 of the Charter:

"[25] The heading which precedes s. 11 of the Charter reads:

'Proceedings In Criminal and Penal Matters'

The portion of s. 11 which determines in the first instance the persons who may derive the benefit thereof is the first line which states: 'Any person charged with an offence has the right ...' The heading to s. 11 makes it clear that s. 11 of the Charter applies to an offence although it may not be, strictly speaking, a criminal matter. It is sufficient if it is a penal matter. Even if contempt proceedings are not properly characterized as criminal matters, there can be no doubt that they must, at the very least, be characterized as penal matters. The word penal, as used in the heading undoubtedly bears the dictionary meaning 'of, pertaining to or relating to punishment'. It is the power to punish for contempt of court which is expressly preserved by s. 8 of the Criminal Code and as previously stated I am of the view that the citation for contempt of court constitutes the charging of an offence within the meaning of s. 11. It follows, therefore, that s. 11(c) and (d) of the Charter are applicable to contempt of court proceedings."

On that basis, a distinction is to be drawn between a criminal proceeding and a proceeding, while not criminal, which is penal in nature. An act may be prohibited by law on pain of punishment. It may or may not be criminal. The character of the act, and of the proceeding, is to be determined upon a proper construction of the legislation giving rise to the charge.

The distinction between proceedings which are criminal in nature and those which are penal in nature was drawn in Donald v. Law Society of British Columbia [1984] 2 W.W.R. 46 (B.C.C.A.). It was argued in that case that s. 13 of the Charter was restricted to criminal proceedings. Evidence had been given by a lawyer in an action for defamation, and the question was whether that evidence could be admitted against him in disciplinary proceedings before a committee of the Law Society. The majority of the Court held that disciplinary proceedings before the benchers ought not to be regarded as criminal proceedings, but as civil in nature. Mr. Justice Anderson, in separate reasons, concurred in the result. He construed the statute, Barristers and Solicitors Act R.S.B.C. 1979, c. 26, and the rules made pursuant to it, and expressed this opinion at p. 59:

"The use of the words 'offence', 'verdict of guilt', 'penalties' and 'punishment' serves to reinforce my opinion that the proceedings against the accused are not 'civil' in the sense that they do not deal with the establishment of civil liability but, rather, with establishment of guilt in respect of a statutory offence. In other words, the proceedings are penal in nature with penal consequences."

In my opinion, the proceedings taken in this case under the Penitentiary Service Regulations for a breach of discipline, while penal in nature, were not criminal proceedings.

Is the appellant an individual who may be imprisoned or confined through civil proceeding?

Mr. Justice Meredith held that he was not, saying, at p. 420-21:

"The present proceedings are therefore not civil in nature as not involving rights as between individuals. Rather the proceedings are disciplinary measures."

He relied upon the distinction made by Cameron, J.A. in R. v. Wigglesworth (supra) at p. 32-33. I agree with that conclusion.

The appellant makes this submission:

... the judgment below restricting the definition of 'civil' as contained in section 3 of the Legal Services Society Act to matters involving a breach of one person's duty to another, involving injury and constituting a 'private cause of action for damages for which the actor must answer to the person injured' relying on R. v. Wigglesworth (supra) is in error because it was clearly the intention of the legislature in drafting section 3, subsection (2), paragraph (b) that the 'civil proceedings' contemplated were civil proceedings through which the person 'may be imprisoned or confined' such as civil commitment proceedings under the Mental Health Act or other similar civil administrative law matters, as opposed to civil actions for damages. It is submitted that the legislative intent with respect to section 3 of the Legal Services Society Act was to ensure that all persons who qualified financially would be provided with legal services if liberty was at risk in proceedings other than 'criminal proceedings' ..."

The respondent agrees that s. 3(2)(b) contemplates a limited class of proceedings such as contempt of court (Rule 56), or committal proceedings (Rule 48). Those are proceedings in the ordinary courts. But the appellant submits that the right to have counsel provided under the Legal Services Society Act extends to all cases, civil in nature, which may involve loss of liberty.

Disciplinary proceedings before the Law Society have been regarded as civil in nature: Re James [1983] 2 W.W.R. 316 (B.C.S.C.), and Donald v. Law Society of British Columbia (supra), although Anderson, J.A. in the latter case held the proceedings to be penal in nature. The appellant relies on those cases. But no cases have been cited to me that holds that proceedings under the Penitentiary Service Regulations are civil in nature. The analogy which is most often made is to criminal rather than civil law.

I am not persuaded that proceedings before the Disciplinary Court are civil proceedings. They are internal proceedings designed to foster order, and do not bear any of the usual characteristics of civil matters.

It should be noted that proceedings involving loss of liberty in a prison, or by confinement in a mental institution, may be civil in nature in cases where judicial review in the ordinary courts is appropriate. But, this is not such a case.

I conclude that the appellant is not an individual who may be imprisoned or confined through civil proceedings.

Is the proceeding under the Penitentiary Service Regulations a legal problem that threatens the appellant's livelihood?

The appellant had been paid a small wage for work done within the prison. If found guilty of the disciplinary offence, he may be confined and unable to work. I do not think his nominal prison earnings of $4.25 a day can be described as a "livelihood". Livelihood is defined as a means of living maintenance, or a means of support. While the appellant is in prison, he earns no livelihood. That is provided for him free of charge. His prison earnings may allow him to purchase extras, but they do not constitute a livelihood.

I conclude that the proceedings against the appellant under the Penitentiary Service Regulations do not fall within the description of matters under s. 3(2) of the Legal Services Society Act which would obligate the Society to provide legal services to the appellant.

I would dismiss the appeal.

I agree. CRAIG J.A.

I agree. CHEFFINS J.A.
